mappings.rationaleAI: Napoleon's rise from First Consul to Emperor, the Napoleonic Code, Concordat with the Church, and administrative centralization directly cover France from 1789 to 1815 (A.1.b.iii, A.1.b.iv). His campaigns at Austerlitz, Jena, Wagram, the Russian campaign, Leipzig, and Waterloo map to the coalitions and international war (A.1.c.i–v). His reshaping of German, Italian, Swiss, and Low Countries political structures covers effects of French occupation (A.1.d).
Washington's leadership in the American Revolution represents the 'patriotism' in America (A.1.a) that formed part of the climate of change preceding the French Revolution.

Covers the Serbian crisis, general mobilization, trench warfare, new military technology, entry of other nations, the Eastern and Western Fronts, submarine warfare, US entry, Russian Revolution, armistice, and the Paris Peace Conference and Treaty of Versailles (B.2.d.i–vi).
The American Revolution (1765–1783) directly represents the 'patriotism' in America that contributed to the climate of change preceding the French Revolution (A.1.a).

The Ottoman Empire's Tanzimat reforms, Young Turk Revolution, World War I participation, and dissolution connect to the Balkan states' emergence from Ottoman control (A.2.s, B.3.k) and the broader reconfiguration of southeastern Europe.

Marx's historical materialism, class struggle theory, and Communist Manifesto represent the prevalence of Determinism and Materialism in philosophy and political and social thought from 1850 to 1920 (B.1.a).

Italy's history during this period spans the French Revolutionary era republics (A.1.l), the Risorgimento and revolutionary movements (A.2.m), the Italian War of Independence and Kingdom establishment (B.2.a.ii), and the unified Kingdom's domestic and foreign policies through World War I (B.3.f).

The rapid European partition of Africa (1880–1914), the Berlin Conference, and colonial administration directly map to the resurgence of European imperialism, new theories of imperialism, and the partitioning of Africa (B.2.c.i–ii).

Russia under Alexander I and the Napoleonic Wars (A.1.f), Nicholas I's conservative policies and the Decembrist revolt (A.2.o), and the Russian Empire from the Crimean War through the 1917 revolution (B.3.e) are all directly covered.

France's history from the Revolution through Napoleon (A.1.b), the Restoration, July Monarchy, and Second Republic (A.2.j), and the Second Empire and Third Republic through World War I (B.3.b) comprehensively spans this section.

Enlightenment political theory — liberty, constitutional government, natural rights — formed the intellectual climate of change preceding the French Revolution (A.1.a) and influenced revolutionary ideology.
Voltaire's advocacy for civil liberties, religious toleration, and reason catalyzed the intellectual climate preceding the French Revolution (A.1.a), introducing English constitutionalism to France.

The formal end of the Holy Roman Empire in 1806 through Napoleon's reorganization of the German states is directly covered (A.1.c.ii), and the Confederation of the Rhine replacing the smaller German states under French influence (A.1.i).

The British Empire's expansion during the Scramble for Africa and Pax Britannica connects to European imperialist expansion (B.2.c), and Great Britain's domestic and overseas developments from 1850 to 1920 (B.3.a) including the South African War and World War I.

The Revolution's progression from the Third Estate revolt through the Convention, Terror, Thermidorian reaction, and Directory directly maps to A.1.b.i–ii. The legacy of the Revolution — cultural nationalism and populism — covers A.2.b.
Locke's natural rights philosophy, government by consent, and right of revolution directly influenced the intellectual climate of 'patriotism' in America that preceded the French Revolution (A.1.a).

Tolstoy's literary works (War and Peace, Anna Karenina) and moral philosophy represent developments in arts, philosophy, and religion from 1850–1920 (B.1.b), while his depiction of Russian society connects to the Russian Empire's cultural and social developments (B.3.e.iii).
Poland's conflict with Russia and the 1863 rebellion connect to Russian foreign policy regarding Poland (A.2.o.ii) and the spread of revolutionary sentiment under Alexander II (B.3.e.i).

Beethoven's career bridging Classical and Romantic styles, his emotional intensity and expanded formal scale, directly exemplifies the Romantic movement's individualism contrasted with the Enlightenment (A.2.c).
Freud's development of psychoanalysis as a behavioral science, his theories of the unconscious and psychosexual development, represent the development of behavioral sciences and new views (B.1.c).

The UK's history covers the influence of the French Revolution and Pitt's ministries (A.1.e), political and social reforms from 1815–1850 (A.2.i), and mid-Victorian through World War I developments including liberalism, imperialism, and the Irish Question (B.3.a).
Theoretical frameworks of imperialism — including economic imperialism and Social Darwinism — directly correspond to renewed interest in overseas expansion and new theories of imperialism (B.2.c.i).

Spain under Charles IV and the Peninsular War (A.1.j), Ferdinand VII and Isabella II's succession disputes (A.2.q.i), and continued civil strife, the First Republic, and colonial losses through the Spanish-American War (B.3.h).

A broad survey of European history covering the French Revolution, industrialization, two World Wars, and Cold War, which spans the entire section's treatment of European political and economic revolution (A) and realism, nationalism, and imperialist expansion (B).
The early modern period's endpoint — the French Revolution and Industrial Revolution onset — directly intersects with the climate of change preceding the French Revolution (A.1.a).

Catherine II's reign ended in 1796, immediately preceding Paul I and Alexander I. Her Enlightenment engagement and imperial expansion provide context for Russia's participation in the Napoleonic Wars (A.1.f).
Nightingale's pioneering nursing during the Crimean War (1853–1856) connects to mid-Victorian Britain's involvement in the Crimean War (B.3.a.i) and Russia's defeat in that war (B.3.e.i).
The abolition of slavery in British colonies (1833) as part of the political and social reform measures of 1822–1848 (A.2.i.ii) connects to the broader Atlantic slave trade and its ending.
Picasso's co-founding of Cubism and modernist innovations represent developments in the arts from 1850 to 1920 (B.1.b).
Washington's leadership in the American Revolution represents the 'patriotism' in America (A.1.a) that formed part of the climate of change preceding the French Revolution.
Spain's loss of its South American empire in the 1820s (A.2.q.i) and further colonial losses in the Spanish-American War of 1898 (B.3.h.i) trace the decline of the Spanish Empire within this period.

The Ottoman Empire's Tanzimat reforms, Young Turk Revolution, World War I participation, and dissolution connect to the Balkan states' emergence from Ottoman control (A.2.s, B.3.k) and the broader reconfiguration of southeastern Europe.

The rapid European partition of Africa (1880–1914), the Berlin Conference, and colonial administration directly map to the resurgence of European imperialism, new theories of imperialism, and the partitioning of Africa (B.2.c.i–ii).

Faraday's discovery of electromagnetic induction and electrolysis connects to scientific developments in Continental countries and their effects on society (A.2.d), and the broader scientific developments from 1850–1920 (B.1.c).
The French expedition to Egypt and Syria (1798–1802) is directly covered under Napoleon's campaigns (A.1.c.ii).
The US War of Independence as part of the climate of change (A.1.a), the US as a new area of industrial expansion (B.1.d.i), and US entry into World War I (B.2.d.iii).
Turkey's Ottoman predecessor is covered under the Balkan states, where the Ottoman Empire's interactions with Serbia, Greece, and Bulgaria shaped southeastern European history (B.3.k).
Poland's conflict with Russia and the 1863 rebellion connect to Russian foreign policy regarding Poland (A.2.o.ii) and the spread of revolutionary sentiment under Alexander II (B.3.e.i).
